Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Dways International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Dways International Pte Ltd was incorporated in November 2018 in Singapore.
At incorporation, Nancy Long was a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran were shareholders.
Dways International Pte Ltd was formerly known as D’way International Pte Ltd and as Longevite Pte Ltd.

Dways International Pte Ltd opened an OCBC bank account in November 2018 with Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran as authorised signatories for cheques and Nancy Long as the sole authorised person for internet banking transactions.
On or around 8 March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene was added as an authorised signatory to Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC bank account.
On 8 March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and the Lim siblings became shareholders around the same period.
From March 2019, the group comprising Nancy Long, Zulkifli bin Othman Curran, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene and Lim Kim Hwa Justin were collectively referred to within the business as the Four Persons.

In or around early 2019, Karen Tang Lee Cheng and Chua Hong Chor Steven were engaged as distributors of Dways International Pte Ltd and assisted with stock-taking and collection of products by other distributors.

In early 2019, the Four Persons discussed roles in Dways International Pte Ltd, including that Irene would handle accounts and operations and that Justin would assist with financial matters, although no written employment agreements were executed.
From early 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in Dways International Pte Ltd's business operations as a substantial shareholder without a formal written employment contract.
Around mid-June 2019, the Four Persons discussed by WhatsApp the printing of name cards for themselves with designations including 'Founder and CFO' for Lim Kim Hwa Justin.

Around mid-June 2019, a document titled 'Order of namecards' was prepared listing the names and designations to be printed on Dways International Pte Ltd name cards, including 'Founder and CFO' for Lim Kim Hwa Justin and 'Founder' for the Lim siblings.
In July 2019, an invoice from a vendor was signed by Nancy Long as 'CEO' and by Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as 'CFO' on behalf of Dways International Pte Ltd.

In or around mid-March 2019, an unsigned partnership agreement draft was circulated stating that Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene would be Chief Financial Officer and Lim Kim Hwa Justin would be Chief Operating Officer of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From March 2019, numerous WhatsApp chat groups were created among the Four Persons and staff to discuss Dways International Pte Ltd's business and operations.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene took charge of making payments for Dways International Pte Ltd, maintained spreadsheets of payments to suppliers and vendors, and updated Nancy Long on bank balances.

From March 2019 to February 2020, the Lim siblings had operational control over Dways International Pte Ltd's bank records and accounts and access to products and sales records, while Nancy Long also retained access to bank accounts and premises.

Until around November 2019, Nancy Long had joint control with Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene over the OCBC bank account of Dways International Pte Ltd, including receiving bank statements at her home until September 2019.
On 24 October 2019, a WhatsApp message from Karen Tang indicated that store and locker keys for Dways International Pte Ltd were kept in an office drawer.
On or about 25 November 2019, Nancy Long signed an OCBC form authorising any one director of Dways International Pte Ltd to apply for account and banking services.

On 20 December 2019, Nancy Long informed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene that she did not have the bank tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's accounts and requested online access; Irene replied that she took charge of accounts and would continue making payments while travelling.

By around 20 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene retained the internet banking tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts and was the only person effecting online transfers and payments.

On or about 25 November 2019, the authorised signatory for Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB bank account was changed to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as sole signatory, with Nancy Long signing a form authorising a single user to transact.

From March 2019, both Nancy Long and Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene were authorised signatories for Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB bank account, and internet banking transactions required approval by both until the signatory change in November 2019.
In October 2019, the mailing address for Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC bank account was changed from Nancy Long's home address to the company's office address.
On 8 November 2019, Karen Tang informed Nancy Long by WhatsApp to bring two sets of Dways International Pte Ltd office keys to be duplicated for new staff.
On 21 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd received stock of the product HL Span from its manufacturer.
On 1 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd received stock of the product Purity from its manufacturer.
On 17 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd received stock of the product B’Glo from its manufacturer, which was stored at the Lim siblings' home at Nancy Long's instruction.
On 28 November 2019, staff member Carol informed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene by WhatsApp that Nancy Long had taken 12 boxes of Purity from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.
On 3 December 2019, Nancy Long took HL Span products from a cabinet at Dways International Pte Ltd's office and recorded the quantities later in the company's books.

On 21 December 2019, Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran took HL Span, Purity and B’Glo products from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock, with quantities later described in WhatsApp messages by Karen Tang.

Throughout 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd used a computer system to record distributor information, product purchases and amounts paid, accessible by Nancy Long, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene and staff.
During 2019, the Chuas conducted stock checks for Dways International Pte Ltd and placed stock records in office drawers.

On 6 January 2020, Karen Tang reported a shortfall of 17 boxes of HL Span in Dways International Pte Ltd's stock, and Nancy Long replied that she had taken them earlier and had written down what she had taken.

On 4 July 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd disbursed a loan of $30,000 to Karen Tang and Chua Hong Chor, with the loan described in a WhatsApp group titled 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' and a stated repayment period of four months.
On 4 July 2019, a message in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group recorded that Dways International Pte Ltd agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven and Karen with repayment in four months.
On 23 September 2019, in the 'DWay Accounts Updates' WhatsApp group, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene reported to the Four Persons that there was a loan amount of S$30,000 to Steven and Karen outstanding.

On 5 December 2019, Nancy Long messaged the Chuas stating that the outstanding S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd was supposed to have been settled in October but remained unpaid in December.
On 6 January 2020, Nancy Long and Lim Kim Hwa Justin discussed repayment of the S$30,000 loan from the Chuas in WhatsApp messages.
On 1 April 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene emailed Nancy Long stating that since the S$30,000 was a Dways loan to the Chuas, Dways should pursue repayment from them.
On 28 July 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed a profit balance of approximately US$699 from an initial investment of S$10,500.

On 31 July 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed a profit balance of approximately US$829, with Justin messaging Nancy Long that the profit was US$829 in less than two weeks for the S$10,500 invested.
On or about 11 November 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed an accumulated profit balance of approximately US$1,527.20.
On 25 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd made a donation of S$1,500 to Psalt Care Ltd for a charity dinner, with the donation discussed in relation to using profits from the TLC investment.
On 1 August 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid S$1,500 to Zulkifli bin Othman Curran, with internal messages referring to using TLC investment profits to fund this payment.

On 20 and 21 July 2019, members of the 'DWay Office Reno' WhatsApp group, including Nancy Long and the Defendants, discussed procuring a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror for Dways International Pte Ltd's office and its cost.
On 31 July 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$11,676.75 for renovation works at its office, including S$2,900 for a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On or around 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$2,340, including S$1,580 for fabrication and installation of a mirror beside an existing folding door.

On 31 December 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$2,220 for works including a table cabinet beside a stage with an additional step and a slide-and-fold door with key lock at a downstairs storeroom.

On 30 August 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$6.80 at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, S$77.55 and S$81.10 at Tim Ho Wan, and S$24.00 at Au Croissant for meals and refreshments for persons assisting with office furniture installation.
On 14 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$20.22 at Crown Coffee for refreshments for the Four Persons.
On 13 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$75.21 at Tim Ho Wan for dinner for the Chuas who assisted in setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 2 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$45.79 at Tim Ho Wan for meals for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$156.10 for refreshments for an Opportunity Product Presentation held on 14 September 2019.
On 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$530.46 for refreshments and meals for directors, shareholders and staff in or around August and September 2019.
On 30 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$230 for engaging a cleaning company to clean the company's premises in about September 2019.
On 4 September 2019, internal messages recorded that Dways International Pte Ltd required its shareholders to top up its accounts due to low balances.
On 27 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a UOB cheque for S$3,000 payable to Yang Tjun Yu, who had carried out website-related work for the company.
On 27 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a UOB cheque for S$1,045.45 payable to employee Norlin as part of her remuneration.
On 18 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a cheque for S$3,959 payable to Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd for corporate merchandise or related services.
On 14 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a cheque for S$3,664.75 payable to Stratgeist Pte Ltd for production of promotional videos.
On or about 30 October 2019, an ACRA business profile search for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd costing S$5.50 was conducted and later reimbursed from Dways International Pte Ltd funds.

Enhance Life International Pte Ltd was incorporated in October 2016 with Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as shareholder and director, and later all Four Persons became shareholders; Nancy Long became a director on 11 June 2019.

In September and October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid CPF contributions for employee Norlin by transferring amounts of S$144 and S$178 to her, with the understanding that she would declare them to the CPF Board.

In October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$36,783.22 for various expenses she had paid on behalf of the company, including staff CPF contributions and other operational costs.
In October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid season parking fees of S$192.60 for the month of October 2019 for Karen Tang, who was assisting substantially at the office.

On 9 January 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$10,210 from its UOB account to a DBS account held by Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, recorded internally as a payment to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.
On 15 January 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$400 from its UOB account to WLP Pte Ltd as accounting fees for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.
On 7 October 2019, a Cosmo System Pte Ltd tax invoice for S$252.51 addressed to Dways International Pte Ltd was posted in a WhatsApp chat group including the Four Persons.
On 12 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid Cosmo System Pte Ltd S$911.64 for cloud server and website domain services.
On 4 December 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid Cosmo System Pte Ltd S$252.51 for cloud server and website domain services.

On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$4,276.84 to Karen Tang as commission and bonuses due to her as a distributor, after deducting a referral bonus related to a refund request by her downline Sandra.

On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$21,000 from its UOB account to an account of Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, comprising S$10,500 for her and S$10,500 for Lim Kim Hwa Justin as allowances.
On 5 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$280 from its OCBC account to WLP Pte Ltd for an invoice describing services for transfer of shares and resignation of a director.
On 5 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$980 to Lionel Lem described as a deposit for preparation of accounts.

On 5 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$1,000 and S$2,000 from its bank accounts to a DBS account of Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, recorded internally as refunds to distributor Srii Wilano.
On 5 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$3,000 to CMS Global in connection with distributor Lionel's request for a refund of products.
On 5 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$3,000 to distributor Sandra Ai Choo in connection with her request for a refund of products.

By about 17 January 2020, the working relationship between the Lim siblings and Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran had deteriorated, and a meeting was held where the possible sale of the Lim siblings' shares in Dways International Pte Ltd was raised.
On 22 January 2020, Nancy Long and Lim Kim Hwa Justin met to discuss the potential sale of the Lim siblings' shares in Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 23 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin went to Dways International Pte Ltd's office and removed products HL Span and Purity from the premises.
On 30 January 2020, the Lim siblings, assisted by the Chuas, went to Dways International Pte Ltd's office and removed products HL Span and Purity from the premises.
On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene ceased to be a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and the Lim siblings ceased to be shareholders.
In February 2020, Karen Tang and Chua Hong Chor ceased to be distributors for Dways International Pte Ltd.

In early February 2020, Nancy Long requested by WhatsApp that Lim Kim Hwa Justin return stock records and updated stock accounts of Dways International Pte Ltd, and later acknowledged receipt of invoices and bank statement records from the Lim siblings.

From around September or October 2019, the Four Persons operated on an understanding that each of them could take a monthly quantity of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo products from Dways International Pte Ltd for personal consumption and for distribution as samples.
From around late 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd used CCTV cameras in and around its office to record movements, including product removals.
On 27 May 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd commenced Suit No 447 of 2020 in the General Division of the High Court of Singapore against the Lim siblings and the Chuas.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based only on pre-dispute conduct and transactions, the Lim siblings and the Chuas removed company funds and products or used company bank accounts and investment profits in a manner inconsistent with Dways International Pte Ltd's documented arrangements and recorded business purposes, and whether the S$30,000 loan and TLC investment profits remained outstanding to the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore company law, directors and persons with control over company assets are apply company funds and property only for proper corporate purposes, to document and justify payments and transfers, and to ensure that loans and investment proceeds belonging to the company are either repaid or properly authorised for alternative use.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute records Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene controlled Dways International Pte Ltd's bank tokens and executed most online payments, while Nancy Long pre-signed cheques and did not routinely review statements. Payments to operational vendors such as Cosmo System Pte Ltd, VSK Design Pte Ltd, Stratgeist Pte Ltd, and Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd are supported by invoices and contemporaneous WhatsApp discussions. CPF-related transfers to employee Norlin align with payroll vouchers. The S$30,000 loan to the Chuas is described in a dedicated WhatsApp group as a Dways loan with a four-month repayment period and is repeatedly referenced as outstanding in later messages, with no contemporaneous record of any write-off. TLC investment wallet screenshots show a single accumulated profit figure of about US$1,527.20 by November 2019, with no subsequent record of that amount being transferred to Dways' bank accounts. In January and early February 2020, as the relationship between the Four Persons deteriorated and the Lim siblings prepared to exit, Dways' bank records show clustered transfers including S$21,000 in allowances to an account of Irene, S$10,210 and S$400 linked to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd, S$980 to a private individual for 'preparation of accounts', and multiple refunds to distributors, while CCTV and WhatsApp evidence record product removals on 23 and 30 January 2020. These events occurred after the Four Persons had already been taking monthly product quantities under an informal entitlement arrangement and while the Lim siblings still had control of the bank tokens.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed whether alleged oral employment agreements existed, concluding that Dways failed to prove the Employment Agreements for Irene and Justin due to lack of documentation, inconsistent dates, and equivocal evidence such as name cards and an unsigned partnership agreement. Nonetheless, the judge found that Irene had in fact agreed with Nancy to be the director primarily in charge of accounts and payments, based on her own admissions, WhatsApp messages, and her signing as 'CFO' on an invoice. The court held that Nancy retained access to the OCBC and UOB accounts until late 2019 and was not excluded from control; she chose not to exercise that control. By December 2019, however, Irene had effectively excluded Nancy from internet banking by retaining the tokens, making Irene a custodial fiduciary of the bank accounts.

On control of products and records, the court found that all Four Persons and the Chuas had access to HL Span and Purity stocks and that B’Glo was stored at the Lim siblings' home with Nancy's knowledge. Inventory and sales records were maintained in a decentralised, honour-based system, with the Chuas conducting stock checks and staff entering data into a shared computer system. The court rejected Dways' claim that the Lim siblings had exclusive control over inventory and sales records or that they failed to hand them over upon exit.

Regarding the Disputed Payments, the court distinguished between payments with legitimate commercial basis and those without. Payments to Cosmo System Pte Ltd were upheld as legitimate IT and hosting services, not duplicative of Scrollless Pte Ltd's services. CPF-related payments to Norlin were accepted as consistent with a temporary arrangement to pay CPF directly to staff before CorpPass activation. Karen's commission payment of S$4,276.84 was found to have been approved by Nancy and properly netted off a referral fee. Payments to VSK Design Pte Ltd for the frame, mirror, cabinet and door were supported by WhatsApp discussions and Nancy's knowledge and acquiescence, and the court rejected the argument that they were unnecessary given Dways' early revenue position. Debit card food expenses for the Chuas and the Four Persons were treated as within Irene's discretion and consistent with other unchallenged meal expenses.

For payments without legitimate basis, the court held that the S$192.60 season parking for Karen lacked evidence of Nancy's authorisation and provided no agreed remuneration basis for the Chuas. The S$5.50 ACRA search, S$10,210 transfer, and S$400 payment to WLP were found to benefit Enhance Life International Pte Ltd rather than Dways, with documentary evidence contradicting Irene's explanation about topping up Enhance Life's DBS balance. The court rejected the alleged June 2019 Agreement on allowances and found no documentary support for the S$21,000 allowances; given the deteriorating relationship and timing, the court concluded that Irene breached no-profit and no-conflict rules and that Justin conspired with her regarding this withdrawal.

The S$980 payment to Lionel for 'preparation of accounts' was found to lack any evidence of engagement or preparatory work, and its timing after Lionel's refund request and the Lim siblings' exit further undermined Irene's explanation. The court scrutinised the purported refund payments to distributors Wilano, Lionel and Sandra, finding the supporting letters suspect and internally inconsistent. Bank slips showed funds going to Irene's account rather than directly to Wilano, and Irene's oral evidence conflicted with the letters' contents, including claims that some distributors had not collected products despite letters referring to returns. The court concluded that these refunds were not bona fide and were part of a pattern of depleting Dways' accounts as the Lim siblings exited.

For several cheques where Irene claimed she could not recall the purpose, the court distinguished between those where payees and context could be inferred (Yang, Norlin, Global Premiums & Gifts, Stratgeist) and those where neither payee nor purpose was identifiable. For the latter, the court held that Irene, as the person in charge of payments, bore the burden of explanation and had not discharged it.

On the S$30,000 loan, the court relied on the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group and subsequent messages to find that the loan was from Dways, not a personal loan from the Lim siblings, and that both directors authorised it. The court rejected the Defendants' assertion that Nancy agreed to write off the loan at a meeting on 14 August 2019, noting the absence of any contemporaneous record and the continued treatment of the amount as outstanding in messages up to April 2020. Accordingly, the Chuas were held liable to repay S$30,000 to Dways.

Regarding the TLC investment, the court found that Nancy had misread the wallet screenshots and that there was a single accumulated profit of US$1,527.20 by November 2019. The court accepted that earlier references to using TLC profits for a S$1,500 payment to Zul and a S$1,500 donation to Psalt would have been reflected before the November balance, leaving US$1,527.20 still in the wallet with no evidence of transfer to Dways. The Lim siblings were therefore held liable to account for US$1,527.20 to Dways.

The court declined to order a full account of all transactions, reasoning that the relevant period was short, Dways had already identified and narrowed the Disputed Payments, and losses were quantifiable from bank records. On conspiracy, the court found insufficient evidence that Justin was involved in most impugned payments but concluded that he conspired with Irene in relation to the S$21,000 allowances, given his expected benefit and the timing relative to their exit and product removals.

On product entitlements, the court evaluated competing versions of an oral arrangement. It rejected Nancy's narrower 'Arrangement' and accepted a broader 'Shareholders’ Entitlement' based on Zul's evidence and WhatsApp records showing Nancy taking quantities consistent with the Lim siblings' version, including B’Glo. The court accepted that part of the entitlement was for personal consumption and part for samples, ultimately fixing a Personal Entitlement of two boxes of HL Span, three boxes of Purity and two boxes of B’Glo per month per shareholder. The court held that entitlement ceased when a person left as shareholder and rejected the alleged 'Family Entitlement' of 120 boxes each of HL Span and Purity for Justin's family as an afterthought lacking documentary support and inconsistent with the state of relations and the absence of any agreed share price at the 22 January 2020 meeting.

The court found that the Lim siblings removed products on 23 and 30 January 2020 with assistance from the Chuas, and that any removal beyond their Personal Entitlement after their decision to exit was unauthorised. The court also addressed defamation and conspiracy claims, analysing WhatsApp messages and letters sent by Irene in April 2020, but those aspects involve post-dispute communications and legal characterisations and were not used as pre-dispute factual features here.

Ultimately, the court held Irene liable for a subset of Disputed Payments lacking legitimate basis, held Justin jointly liable for the S$21,000 allowances and US$1,527.20 TLC profits, and held the Chuas liable for repayment of the S$30,000 loan. The court dismissed claims that Justin was a custodial fiduciary or that he conspired in all impugned transactions, and it rejected Dways' broader claims of exclusive control, failure to hand over records, and misappropriation of all product revenues.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene

Facts.Text: On 8 March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became a director of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene served as a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and was internally described as being in charge of accounts and operations.
Around March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene managed Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts, prepared spreadsheets of payments to suppliers and vendors, and updated other shareholders on account balances.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene created and administered numerous WhatsApp chat groups for Dways International Pte Ltd's business, including groups for accounts updates, office renovation, and loans.
Around end March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene was added as an authorised signatory to Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC bank account.
On or about 25 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became the sole authorised signatory for Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB bank account.
By 20 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene held the internet banking tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC and UOB accounts and was the only person executing online transfers and payments.
In July 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene signed a vendor invoice on behalf of Dways International Pte Ltd using the designation 'CFO'.
In mid-March 2019, a draft partnership agreement identified Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as Chief Financial Officer of Dways International Pte Ltd.

On 20 and 21 July 2019, in the 'DWay Office Reno' WhatsApp group, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene discussed with Nancy Long and others the installation and cost of a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror for the office.

On 31 July 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$11,676.75 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd for renovation works including a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.

On or around 23 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$2,340 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd, including S$1,580 for a mirror beside an existing folding door.

On 31 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$2,220 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd for a table cabinet, additional stage step, and a slide-and-fold storeroom door.
On 12 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused Dways International Pte Ltd to pay S$911.64 to Cosmo System Pte Ltd for IT services.
On 4 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused Dways International Pte Ltd to pay S$252.51 to Cosmo System Pte Ltd for IT services.
In September and October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene reimbursed herself S$144 and S$178 from Dways International Pte Ltd for CPF contributions she had paid directly to employee Norlin.
In October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene received a reimbursement of S$36,783.22 from Dways International Pte Ltd for various expenses she had incurred on its behalf.
In October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to pay S$192.60 for season parking for Karen Tang for the month of October 2019.
On 30 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged reimbursement of S$230 from Dways International Pte Ltd to herself for cleaning services for the office.
On 23 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged reimbursement of S$156.10 and S$530.46 from Dways International Pte Ltd to herself for refreshments and meals related to company activities.
On 30 August 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay for meals at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, Tim Ho Wan, and Au Croissant for persons assisting with office work.
On 14 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$20.22 at Crown Coffee for refreshments for the Four Persons.

On 13 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$75.21 at Tim Ho Wan for dinner for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 2 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$45.79 at Tim Ho Wan for meals for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 27 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,000 to Yang Tjun Yu for website-related work.
On 27 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$1,045.45 to employee Norlin as part of her remuneration.
On 18 October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,959 to Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd.
On 14 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,664.75 to Stratgeist Pte Ltd for promotional video production.

On or about 30 October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene conducted an ACRA business profile search on Enhance Life International Pte Ltd costing S$5.50 and later reimbursed this amount from Dways International Pte Ltd.
Since October 2016, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene has been a shareholder and director of Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 9 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$10,210 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to her DBS account, recorded internally as a payment to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 15 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$400 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to WLP Pte Ltd for accounting services for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 1 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$21,000 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to her personal account, allocating S$10,500 to herself and S$10,500 to Lim Kim Hwa Justin as allowances.
On 1 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$4,276.84 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd to Karen Tang as commission and bonuses.

On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$280 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC account to WLP Pte Ltd for an invoice describing transfer of shares and resignation of a director.

On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$980 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd to her acquaintance Lionel Lem described as a non-refundable deposit for preparation of accounts.

On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$1,000 and S$2,000 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts to her DBS account, recorded internally as refunds to distributor Srii Wilano.
On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$3,000 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd to CMS Global in connection with distributor Lionel's refund request.
On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$3,000 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd to distributor Sandra Ai Choo in connection with her refund request.

From around September or October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene participated in an informal arrangement among the Four Persons allowing each to take monthly quantities of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo for personal consumption and sampling.
On 23 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Lim Kim Hwa Justin to remove HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.
On 30 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, together with Lim Kim Hwa Justin and assisted by the Chuas, removed HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.
On 5 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene ceased to be a director and shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and transactions, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used her control over Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts and product stocks to execute payments and removals that were inconsistent with the company's documented business purposes and informal entitlement arrangements, and whether she left outstanding obligations such as the S$30,000 loan and TLC investment profits unreturned to the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and persons controlling company bank accounts are apply company funds only for the company's purposes, to avoid using company assets for unrelated entities or personal benefit without clear authorisation, and to ensure that any product entitlements or allowances are grounded in agreed arrangements and properly documented.
Party_Details.Application: The evidence shows that from March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene managed Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC and UOB accounts, held the internet banking tokens, and executed most payments. Many payments she arranged, such as those to Cosmo System Pte Ltd, VSK Design Pte Ltd, Stratgeist Pte Ltd, Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd, and Yang Tjun Yu, are supported by invoices and contemporaneous WhatsApp discussions about office renovation, IT infrastructure, and marketing. Reimbursements for CPF contributions to Norlin align with payroll vouchers and a temporary arrangement to pay CPF directly to staff. However, certain transfers she initiated, including S$10,210 and S$400 linked to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd, S$5.50 for an ACRA search on Enhance Life, S$192.60 for Karen's season parking, S$980 to a friend for 'preparation of accounts', and clustered refunds to distributors routed through her own account, lack contemporaneous documentation of company benefit or clear authorisation from the other director. The S$21,000 allowances transfer on 1 February 2020 occurred when relations with the other shareholders had deteriorated and shortly after product removals on 23 and 30 January 2020, with no written record of any fixed allowance agreement. WhatsApp records the S$30,000 loan to the Chuas was treated as a Dways loan and remained outstanding, and TLC wallet screenshots show US$1,527.20 in accumulated profits with no subsequent transfer to Dways' bank accounts. These patterns while many of Irene's payments were operational, a subset of late-period transfers and product removals diverged from the company's established documentation and informal entitlement practices.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court held that although Dways did not prove a formal Employment Agreement appointing Irene as director in charge of accounts and operations, Irene's own admissions and conduct established that she had assumed primary responsibility for accounts and payments. She was therefore treated as a custodial fiduciary of the bank accounts. The judge found that Irene had joint control of the OCBC and UOB accounts with Nancy until late 2019, after which she effectively excluded Nancy from internet banking by retaining the tokens. This increased her responsibility for ensuring that payments were for proper corporate purposes.

In assessing the Disputed Payments, the court distinguished between those with clear commercial justification and those without. Payments to Cosmo System Pte Ltd were supported by invoices and contemporaneous WhatsApp messages, and the court rejected Nancy's assertion that Cosmo's services duplicated those of Scrollless Pte Ltd. CPF-related reimbursements to Irene for payments to Norlin were accepted as legitimate, given vouchers showing salary plus CPF and the absence of CPF Board records for that period, consistent with Irene's explanation of a temporary arrangement.

The court accepted that Karen's commission payment of S$4,276.84 was authorised by Nancy and properly netted off a referral fee after Sandra's refund. Payments to VSK Design Pte Ltd for the frame, mirror, cabinet and door were upheld because WhatsApp messages showed Nancy's awareness and lack of objection, and the items were visibly installed at the office. Debit card food expenses for the Chuas and the Four Persons were found to be within an accepted practice of charging reasonable meal expenses to the company, as evidenced by other unchallenged meal payments.

Conversely, the court found that the S$192.60 season parking payment for Karen lacked evidence of Nancy's authorisation and was not part of any agreed remuneration for the Chuas. The S$5.50 ACRA search, S$10,210 transfer, and S$400 payment to WLP were found to benefit Enhance Life International Pte Ltd rather than Dways, with bank statements contradicting Irene's claim that the S$10,210 was needed to avoid a 'fall below' fee. The court noted that the S$10,210 was transferred to Irene's personal DBS account and that Enhance Life's DBS balance already exceeded S$10,000 at year-end 2019.

The court rejected Irene's claim of a June 2019 Agreement on allowances, noting the absence of any written record despite extensive WhatsApp usage and the informal nature of Dways' operations. It found that the S$21,000 allowances transfer on 1 February 2020 was not supported by prior approvals or consistent practice and occurred when relations had soured and the Lim siblings were preparing to exit. The judge concluded that Irene breached the no-profit and no-conflict rules and that Justin conspired with her in relation to this withdrawal, given his expected benefit and knowledge.

The S$980 payment to Lionel for 'preparation of accounts' was found to be unsupported by any engagement letter, work product, or contemporaneous messages. Its timing, after Lionel's refund request and near the Lim siblings' exit, further undermined Irene's explanation. The court scrutinised the purported refund payments to distributors Wilano, Lionel and Sandra, finding the supporting letters internally inconsistent and their authenticity doubtful. It noted that bank slips showed funds going to Irene's account rather than directly to Wilano, and that Irene's oral evidence conflicted with the letters, including claims that some distributors had not collected products despite letters referring to returns. The court concluded that these refunds were not bona fide and were part of a pattern of depleting Dways' accounts as Irene exited.

For several cheques where Irene claimed she could not recall the purpose, the court held that as the director in charge of payments, she bore the burden of explanation. Where payees and context could be inferred (Yang, Norlin, Global Premiums & Gifts, Stratgeist), the court accepted legitimate purposes. Where neither payee nor purpose was identifiable, the court treated the payments as lacking legitimate basis.

On the S$30,000 loan, the court found that Irene's own WhatsApp messages described it as a Dways loan to the Chuas with a four-month repayment period. Subsequent messages from Irene and Nancy treated the amount as outstanding, and Irene's April 2020 email stated that Dways should pursue repayment. The court rejected the Defendants' claim that Nancy agreed to write off the loan, noting the absence of any contemporaneous record of such an agreement.

Regarding the TLC investment, the court accepted that the wallet screenshots showed a single accumulated profit of US$1,527.20 by November 2019 and that earlier references to using TLC profits for a S$1,500 payment to Zul and a S$1,500 donation to Psalt would have been reflected before that balance. There was no evidence that the US$1,527.20 was transferred to Dways, so Irene and Justin were held liable to account for it.

On product entitlements, the court accepted that an informal 'Shareholders’ Entitlement' existed, allowing each of the Four Persons to take monthly quantities of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo for personal consumption and sampling. It rejected Nancy's narrower 'Arrangement' and the Lim siblings' later-asserted 'Family Entitlement' of 120 boxes each of HL Span and Purity for Justin's family, finding the latter an afterthought prompted by CCTV evidence of product removals. The court fixed a Personal Entitlement of two boxes of HL Span, three boxes of Purity and two boxes of B’Glo per month per shareholder and held that entitlement ceased when a person left as shareholder. It found that the Lim siblings' removals on 23 and 30 January 2020 exceeded any remaining entitlement and were unauthorised.

Overall, the court held Irene liable for a defined subset of Disputed Payments lacking legitimate basis, for misapplication of funds linked to Enhance Life, for the S$21,000 allowances (jointly with Justin), and for failing to account for TLC profits, while also treating her as a key actor in the unauthorised product removals.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Kim Hwa Justin

Facts.Text: Around March 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin became a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From early 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in Dways International Pte Ltd's business operations as a substantial shareholder without a formal written employment contract.
In mid-March 2019, a draft partnership agreement identified Lim Kim Hwa Justin as Chief Operating Officer of Dways International Pte Ltd.
Around mid-June 2019, a name card order document listed Lim Kim Hwa Justin's designation as 'Founder and CFO' of Dways International Pte Ltd.

Throughout 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in multiple WhatsApp chat groups discussing Dways International Pte Ltd's business, including groups on accounts updates, office renovation, and executive matters.

On 28 July 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin sent Nancy Long a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showing a profit of about US$699 and suggested using profits to pay S$1,500 to Zulkifli without using company funds.

On 31 July 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin sent Nancy Long a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet showing a profit of about US$829 and described it as 'Profit U$829 in less than 2 weeks for DWAY888 invested 10,500'.
On or about 11 November 2019, the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed an accumulated profit of about US$1,527.20, which remained in the wallet.
On 1 August 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid S$1,500 to Zulkifli bin Othman Curran, with internal messages from Lim Kim Hwa Justin referring to using TLC investment profits for this payment.

On 25 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd made a S$1,500 donation to Psalt Care Ltd for a charity dinner, with Lim Kim Hwa Justin involved in discussions about funding this from TLC investment profits.
On 23 September 2019, in the 'DWay Accounts Updates' WhatsApp group, Lim Kim Hwa Justin received an update from Irene stating that there was a S$30,000 loan amount to Steven and Karen outstanding.

On 4 July 2019, in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group, Lim Kim Hwa Justin was included in messages recording that Dways International Pte Ltd agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven and Karen with repayment in four months.
On 6 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin discussed with Nancy Long by WhatsApp the repayment of the S$30,000 loan from the Chuas.
On 22 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin met Nancy Long to discuss the potential sale of the Lim siblings' shares in Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 23 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin went to Dways International Pte Ltd's office and removed HL Span and Purity products from the premises.
On 30 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin, together with Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene and assisted by the Chuas, removed HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.

On 1 February 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin was allocated S$10,500 from a S$21,000 transfer that Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused to be made from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to her personal account as allowances for both of them.

From around September or October 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in an informal arrangement among the Four Persons allowing each to take monthly quantities of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo for personal consumption and sampling.
On 5 February 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin ceased to be a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and transactions, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in or benefited from payments and product removals from Dways International Pte Ltd that were inconsistent with the company's documented purposes and informal entitlement arrangements, and whether he left TLC investment profits outstanding to the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Shareholders and de facto officers who participate in company financial decisions are expected not to collaborate in or benefit from transfers of company funds or products that lack a proper corporate basis, and to ensure that investment profits belonging to the company are returned or applied with clear company authorisation.
Party_Details.Application: The records Lim Kim Hwa Justin was a substantial shareholder involved in Dways International Pte Ltd's operations and communications, including TLC investment management and discussions about the S$30,000 loan to the Chuas. He shared TLC wallet screenshots with Nancy Long and proposed using profits to fund payments to Zulkifli and a donation to Psalt Care Ltd. By November 2019, the TLC wallet showed US$1,527.20 in accumulated profits with no subsequent transfer to Dways' bank accounts. In January 2020, Justin met Nancy to discuss selling the Lim siblings' shares and, on 23 and 30 January 2020, removed HL Span and Purity products from the office with Irene and the Chuas. On 1 February 2020, he was allocated S$10,500 from a S$21,000 allowances transfer initiated by Irene from Dways' UOB account, despite the absence of any written allowance agreement and the deteriorating relationship with the other shareholders. These events Justin participated in and benefited from the allowances withdrawal and shared responsibility for ensuring TLC profits were accounted for, while his involvement in other impugned payments was less directly evidenced.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court found that Dways did not prove the existence of a formal Employment Agreement appointing Justin as Chief Financial Officer, noting the lack of documentation, inconsistent dates, and equivocal evidence such as name cards and the unsigned partnership agreement. It held that Justin was not a custodial fiduciary because he did not have control over Dways' bank tokens or formal authority to execute payments. However, the court recognised that he was a substantial shareholder who participated in operations and communications, including TLC investment management and discussions about the S$30,000 loan.

On the Disputed Payments, the court concluded that Dways had not shown that Justin caused or procured most of the impugned transactions or that he conspired with Irene in relation to them, given the absence of evidence that he operated the bank accounts or instructed specific payments. The judge accepted that Irene primarily handled payments and that Nancy pre-signed cheques for convenience. Nonetheless, the court found that Justin conspired with Irene regarding the S$21,000 allowances withdrawal on 1 February 2020. It reasoned that Justin stood to benefit from S$10,500 of the transfer, that the withdrawal occurred when relations had deteriorated and shortly after product removals, and that there was no documentary support for any allowance agreement. The court inferred that Irene made the withdrawal with Justin's agreement and knowledge.

Regarding the TLC investment, the court analysed wallet screenshots and WhatsApp messages between Justin and Nancy. It determined that Nancy had misread the screenshots and that there was a single accumulated profit of US$1,527.20 by November 2019. The court accepted that earlier references to using TLC profits for a S$1,500 payment to Zulkifli and a S$1,500 donation to Psalt would have been reflected before the November balance. As there was no evidence that the US$1,527.20 was transferred to Dways, the court held Justin and Irene jointly liable to account for that amount.

On the S$30,000 loan, the court noted that Justin was part of the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group where Irene recorded that Dways agreed to loan S$30,000 to the Chuas with repayment in four months. Subsequent messages, including Justin's discussions with Nancy on 6 January 2020, treated the loan as outstanding. The court rejected the Defendants' claim that Nancy agreed to write off the loan, citing the absence of any contemporaneous record and Irene's April 2020 email stating that Dways should pursue repayment. The court held the Chuas, not Justin, liable for repayment.

On product entitlements, the court accepted that an informal 'Shareholders’ Entitlement' existed and rejected the later-asserted 'Family Entitlement' of 120 boxes each of HL Span and Purity for Justin's family as an afterthought prompted by CCTV evidence of product removals. It found no documentary support for such an agreement and noted that at the 22 January 2020 meeting, no share price had even been agreed. The court fixed a Personal Entitlement of two boxes of HL Span, three boxes of Purity and two boxes of B’Glo per month per shareholder and held that entitlement ceased when a person left as shareholder. It concluded that the Lim siblings' removals on 23 and 30 January 2020 exceeded any remaining entitlement and were unauthorised, though the judgment's detailed quantification of misappropriated products and any resulting liability for Justin appears in later sections beyond the excerpt.

Overall, the court limited Justin's financial liability to the S$21,000 allowances (jointly with Irene) and the US$1,527.20 TLC profits, while rejecting broader claims that he was a custodial fiduciary or that he conspired in all of Irene's impugned payments.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tang Lee Cheng (Karen)

Facts.Text: In early 2019, Tang Lee Cheng, also known as Karen, was engaged as a distributor of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From early 2019, Karen assisted Dways International Pte Ltd with stock-taking and collection of products by other distributors.
During 2019, Karen assisted Nancy Long with preparing compensation plans for distributors, communication materials, and arranging appointments with vendors for Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 8 November 2019, Karen informed Nancy Long by WhatsApp to bring two sets of Dways International Pte Ltd office keys to be duplicated for new staff.
On 24 October 2019, Karen sent a WhatsApp message stating that store and locker keys for Dways International Pte Ltd were kept in an office drawer.
On 28 November 2019, Karen relayed to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene that Nancy Long had taken 12 boxes of Purity from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.

On 21 and 22 December 2019, Karen sent WhatsApp messages to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene listing quantities of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo taken by Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.
In October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid S$192.60 for season parking for Karen, who was assisting substantially at the office.

On 30 August 2019, meals for Karen and Steven while they were installing furniture at Dways International Pte Ltd's office were paid using the company's debit card at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, Tim Ho Wan, and Au Croissant.
On 13 September 2019, dinner for Karen and Steven at Tim Ho Wan, after they assisted with setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation, was paid using Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card.
On 2 November 2019, meals for Karen and Steven at Tim Ho Wan, after they assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation, were paid using Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card.

On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$4,276.84 to Karen as commission and bonuses due to her as a distributor, after deducting a referral bonus related to a refund request by her downline Sandra.

On 31 July 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd, a company owned by Steven, received S$11,676.75 from Dways International Pte Ltd for renovation works at its office, including a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On or around 23 September 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,340 from Dways International Pte Ltd, including S$1,580 for a mirror beside an existing folding door.
On 31 December 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,220 from Dways International Pte Ltd for a table cabinet, additional stage step, and a slide-and-fold storeroom door.
On 4 July 2019, Karen and Steven received a S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd, recorded in a WhatsApp group as a Dways loan with a four-month repayment period.
On 4 July 2019, Karen acknowledged in a WhatsApp message the receipt of a S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd to her and Steven.
During 2019, Karen conducted stock checks for Dways International Pte Ltd and placed stock records in office drawers.
On 30 January 2020, Karen assisted the Lim siblings in removing HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.
In February 2020, Karen ceased to be a distributor for Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and transactions, Tang Lee Cheng (Karen) received company funds and participated in product removals and renovation transactions in a manner that left a S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd outstanding and contributed to disputed uses of company resources.
Party_Details.Rule: Distributors and related parties who receive loans and payments from a company are repay loans according to agreed terms and to ensure that any benefits from company-funded renovations or commissions are grounded in clear arrangements and do not leave outstanding obligations to the company.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute evidence shows that Karen acted as a distributor and operational helper for Dways International Pte Ltd, receiving meal expenses, season parking, and a commission payment of S$4,276.84 that Nancy Long had approved. Renovation payments to VSK Design Pte Ltd, owned by Steven, were supported by WhatsApp discussions and visible office works. On 4 July 2019, Karen and Steven received a S$30,000 loan from Dways, recorded in a dedicated WhatsApp group as a Dways loan with a four-month repayment period. Subsequent messages from Nancy and Irene in late 2019 and early 2020 continued to describe the amount as outstanding, and there is no contemporaneous record of any write-off. Karen also assisted the Lim siblings in removing products from the office on 30 January 2020. These facts while some benefits she received, such as commissions and renovation-related work, were tied to company operations, the S$30,000 loan remained unpaid to Dways.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court accepted that Karen was more than a mere distributor and that she assisted with stock-taking, administrative tasks, and event support for Dways. It found that meal expenses and the S$4,276.84 commission payment to her were legitimate and, in the case of the commission, expressly approved by Nancy. Renovation payments to VSK Design Pte Ltd, owned by Steven, were upheld as legitimate because WhatsApp messages showed Nancy's awareness and lack of objection, and the works were visibly installed at the office.

On the S$30,000 loan, the court relied on the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group, where Irene recorded that 'DWay has agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven Karen on 4 July 2019' with repayment in four months, and a subsequent message requesting the Chuas to acknowledge receipt of the loan from 'DWay to you'. The court rejected Nancy's claim that she thought the loan was from the Lim siblings personally, noting the clarity of the group name and messages. It concluded that the loan was from Dways and authorised by both directors.

The court then examined whether the loan had been written off. It found no contemporaneous record of any write-off agreement, despite the Defendants' assertion that Nancy agreed to this at a meeting on 14 August 2019. Instead, messages from 23 September 2019, 5 December 2019, and 6 January 2020, as well as Irene's 1 April 2020 email, consistently treated the S$30,000 as outstanding. The court considered the Defendants' explanation that Nancy had later acknowledged a write-off to be a bare assertion and not credible, especially given their failure to document such an important decision despite their practice of recording key matters in WhatsApp chats.

Accordingly, the court held that the S$30,000 loan remained due and owing from Karen and Steven to Dways and found them jointly and severally liable for repayment. The court also noted Karen's role in assisting the Lim siblings with product removals on 30 January 2020, which formed part of the misappropriation of products analysis, but her primary financial liability in the judgment related to the outstanding loan.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chua Hong Chor (Steven)

Facts.Text: In early 2019, Chua Hong Chor, also known as Steven, was engaged as a distributor of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From early 2019, Steven assisted Dways International Pte Ltd with stock-taking and collection of products by other distributors.
Steven owns VSK Design Pte Ltd, a company that provided renovation services to Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 31 July 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$11,676.75 from Dways International Pte Ltd for renovation works at its office, including a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On or around 23 September 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,340 from Dways International Pte Ltd, including S$1,580 for a mirror beside an existing folding door.
On 31 December 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,220 from Dways International Pte Ltd for a table cabinet, additional stage step, and a slide-and-fold storeroom door.
On 4 July 2019, Steven and Karen received a S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd, recorded in a WhatsApp group as a Dways loan with a four-month repayment period.
On 4 July 2019, Steven was included in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group where the S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd to him and Karen was recorded.
On 30 August 2019, Steven assisted with installing furniture at Dways International Pte Ltd's office, during which meals were paid using the company's debit card.
On 13 September 2019, Steven assisted with setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation for Dways International Pte Ltd, after which dinner was paid using the company's debit card.
On 2 November 2019, Steven assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation for Dways International Pte Ltd, after which meals were paid using the company's debit card.
On 30 January 2020, Steven assisted the Lim siblings in removing HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.
In February 2020, Steven ceased to be a distributor for Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and transactions, Chua Hong Chor (Steven) received a S$30,000 loan and renovation payments from Dways International Pte Ltd in circumstances that left the loan outstanding and contributed to disputed uses of company resources.
Party_Details.Rule: Related-party recipients of company loans and payments are repay loans according to agreed terms and to ensure that renovation and other payments reflect genuine services without leaving unresolved financial obligations to the company.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute evidence shows that Steven, through VSK Design Pte Ltd, provided renovation services to Dways International Pte Ltd and received payments supported by invoices and WhatsApp discussions about specific works. On 4 July 2019, Steven and Karen received a S$30,000 loan from Dways, recorded in a dedicated WhatsApp group as a Dways loan with a four-month repayment period. Subsequent messages from Irene and Nancy in late 2019 and early 2020 continued to describe the amount as outstanding, and Irene's April 2020 email stated that Dways should pursue repayment from the Chuas. There is no contemporaneous record of any agreement to write off the loan. Steven also assisted the Lim siblings in removing products from the office on 30 January 2020. These facts while renovation payments corresponded to actual works, the S$30,000 loan remained unpaid to Dways.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court accepted that VSK Design Pte Ltd, owned by Steven, carried out renovation works for Dways and that payments for the frame, mirror, cabinet and door were supported by invoices and WhatsApp messages showing Nancy's awareness and lack of objection. It rejected Dways' argument that these items were unnecessary or not in the company's best interests, noting that the company was setting up its premises and that Nancy did not object at the time despite being aware of the costs.

On the S$30,000 loan, the court relied on the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group, where Irene recorded that Dways agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven and Karen with repayment in four months, and a subsequent message requesting them to acknowledge receipt of the loan from 'DWay to you'. The court found Nancy's claim that she thought the loan was from the Lim siblings personally to be implausible given the clarity of the messages. It concluded that the loan was from Dways and authorised by both directors.

The court then considered whether the loan had been written off. It found no contemporaneous record of any write-off agreement, despite the Defendants' assertion that Nancy agreed to this at a meeting on 14 August 2019. Instead, messages from 23 September 2019, 5 December 2019, and 6 January 2020, as well as Irene's 1 April 2020 email, consistently treated the S$30,000 as outstanding. The court viewed the Defendants' explanation that Nancy later acknowledged a write-off as a bare assertion and not credible, especially given their practice of recording important matters in WhatsApp chats.

Accordingly, the court held that the S$30,000 loan remained due and owing from Steven and Karen to Dways and found them jointly and severally liable for repayment. The court also noted Steven's role in assisting with product removals on 30 January 2020, which formed part of the misappropriation of products analysis, but his primary financial liability in the judgment related to the outstanding loan.
